text: Day of vengeance, (Calmet) when the Lord shall judge, (1 Corinthians 5:2.) and Egypt shall fall a prey to the most powerful nation of the Chaldeans. (Worthington)
Ethiopia. Hebrew, "Chus," denoting part of Arabia, or rather Ethiopia.

Lydia. Hebrew, "Phut," near Egypt. (Calmet)<hr>These troops were paid. (Haydock)<hr>Chub, in Mareotis.<hr>Covenant: the Jews or auxiliaries. Septuagint add, "the Persians and Cretans." (Calmet)<hr>There were Persians in Africa who followed Hercules. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 5:8.)
Messengers; the Chaldeans. (Calmet)<hr>The Nile was navigable as far as Syene. (St. Jerome)
Dry. The canals were obstructed (Calmet) which rendered cavalry useless. (Herodotus 2:108.)

Memphis, the capital of Lower Egypt, (Calmet) where the idol or bull (Haydock) Apis was fed. (Calmet)<hr>Here Amasis overcame Apries. (Herodotus 2:168.)<hr>The Chaldeans made war on the gods as well as on the people.<hr>Prince Amasis was tributary to Babylon. Some of this probably regards the times of Cambyses and Ochus. (Usher, the year of the world 3478 and 3653.) (Calmet) See Ezechiel 29:14. (Haydock)

Taphnis, or Tanis, famous for the miracles of Moses. (Calmet)<hr>Alexandria. In the Hebrew No, which was the ancient name of that city, which was afterwards rebuilt by Alexander the Great, and from his name called Alexandria. (Challoner)<hr>Septuagint, "Memphis or Diospolis;" (Calmet) or Thebes, capital of Higher Egypt; (Bochart) though it seems rather a maritime town, Nahum 3:8. (Calmet)
Pelusium. Hebrew Sin, (Haydock) "muddy," has the same import as pelos. (Calmet)<hr>The place is now called Damietta. (Haydock)
Darkened: war and misery shall ensue.<hr>Sceptres, by dethroning Apries.<hr>Pride; overflowing of the Nile. See Jeremias 49:19.

Year, in which Jerusalem was taken. Pharao lost a battle. His country was afterwards invaded, (Calmet) and his subjects rebelled, ver. 21. (Haydock)<hr>Part of the kingdom had been already subdued, 4 Kings 24:7. (Worthington)
